draft.introduction: Ephesians 5:1–20 calls believers to be “imitators of God” and to walk as children of light. The passage moves from ethical exhortation to a theology of transformation through exposure to divine light. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: Therefore be imitators of God, as beloved children. And walk in love, as Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God.
translation.original_text: γινεσθε ουν μιμηται του θεου ως τεκνα αγαπητα

translation.text: For at one time you were darkness, but now you are light in the Lord. Walk as children of light, for the fruit of light is found in all that is good and right and true. Therefore it says, “Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you.”
translation.original_text: ητε γαρ ποτε σκοτος νυν δε φως εν κυριω

translator_notes.note: The command to be “imitators of God” (mimetai tou theou, μιμηταί του θεου) is one of the boldest ethical imperatives in the New Testament. It presupposes that human beings can pattern their behavior on divine character—an assumption central to both the imitatio Dei tradition in Judaism and the theosis tradition in Christianity.
